content:
Case 10:
Initial presentation:
- Age: 21
- Sex: female
- Chief complaint: Post-induction consultation for T-ALL with prior sinus vein thrombosis and intracerebral hemorrhage
- Vital signs: Blood pressure 122/74 mmHg, heart rate 96/min regular, respiratory rate 15/min, temperature 37.3 °C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air.
Setting: Outpatient hematology clinic (post-induction consultation)
Capabilities: You are evaluating a clinically stable patient after completion of induction therapy. You can review history and examination findings, request routine laboratory evaluations, and arrange standard imaging and specialist consultations appropriate to an ambulatory setting. More invasive procedures and specialized analyses usually require additional planning.
Provide either a request for a single information item or solve.

response.request: Detailed oncology history and induction regimen/course (T-ALL subtype/risk features, induction protocol used, response status including MRD if available, complications timeline including sinus vein thrombosis/ICH management, current medications including anticoagulation, and planned next therapy).

content:
History Physical:
2025-06-29: The patient presents following completion of induction chemotherapy for T-ALL, seeking discussion regarding further treatment options. During induction, she developed a sinus vein thrombosis complicated by a small left-hemispheric intracerebral hemorrhage. Neurological sequelae include mild right arm weakness and impaired fine motor skills, for which neurological rehabilitation is planned. She remains uncertain about continuing intensive therapy and expresses consideration of treatment discontinuation. On examination, her general condition is good; she is alert, oriented, and cooperative. Skin and mucous membranes are pale. Several puncture sites on the left hand show minor erythema and superficial inflammation, consistent with previous peripheral venous catheter insertions. No palpable lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiopulmonary examination: Normal heart sounds, no murmurs; lungs clear to auscultation. Abdomen soft and non-tender. Neurological examination: Cranial nerves intact; mild weakness and reduced grip strength in the right arm, otherwise full strength with normal reflexes, preserved coordination, and intact sensation.
Current medication: Levothyroxine 75 µg 1-0-0, leuprorelin monthly, enoxaparin 40 mg s.c. q12h, co-trimoxazole 960 mg 1-0-0 (Mon/Wed/Fri), valaciclovir 500 mg 1-0-0, vitamin D supplementation. Self-medication with selenium and omega-3 fatty acids.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "CBC with differential and platelet count (plus basic coagulation if available: PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen)."}}

response.request: CBC with differential and platelet count (plus basic coagulation if available: PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen).

content:
Cbc Chemistry:
Hb 9.4 g/dL, WBC 3.3 ×10⁹/L, Plt 122 ×10⁹/L; ANC 2.4 ×10⁹/L, lymphocytes 0.6 ×10⁹/L, monocytes 0.3 ×10⁹/L; no peripheral blasts detected.
Coagulation: INR 1.4, aPTT 31 sec, D-dimer 1850 µg/L FEU.
Na 137 mmol/L, K 3.7 mmol/L, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73 m²), AST 86 U/L, ALT 91 U/L, bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL, LDH 285 U/L, uric acid 5.6 mg/dL.

Echocardiogram: Normal chamber dimensions and preserved systolic function (LVEF 61%), no wall motion abnormalities, valves structurally and functionally normal, no pericardial effusion.

solve.working_diagnosis: T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia post-induction (apparent hematologic remission peripherally) complicated by cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with secondary small intracerebral hemorrhage; currently on therapeutic LMWH with residual mild right arm weakness
differentials: Persistent/relapsed T-ALL (requires marrow morphology and MRD to confirm remission)
Anticoagulation-related bleeding tendency vs thrombocytopenia contributing to prior ICH
Catheter-associated or infection-associated thrombosis/inflammation at puncture sites
Hepatotoxicity from chemotherapy or concomitant medications causing transaminitis

treatment_plan.action: Obtain post-induction response assessment urgently: bone marrow aspirate/biopsy with flow cytometry and MRD (plus cytogenetics/molecular if not already completed) and document CNS status per protocol

Continue anticoagulation for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with hematology/neurology co-management; reassess intensity/dose and duration based on platelet count, bleeding risk, and interval neuroimaging when available; avoid NSAIDs/omega-3 supplements given bleeding risk

Shared decision-making counseling regarding continuation of intensive therapy (consolidation per T-ALL protocol ± allogeneic HSCT depending on risk/MRD), including fertility/ovarian suppression, neuro-rehab planning, psychosocial support, and discussion of prognosis with/without further therapy

Maintain infection prophylaxis per ALL protocol (TMP-SMX, valacyclovir) and monitor CBC/chemistry weekly during recovery; evaluate and manage transaminitis (review meds, hepatitis/CMV/EBV as indicated, consider imaging if persistent)

Address anemia and cytopenias supportively (iron/B12/folate evaluation, transfusion as needed), and treat superficial puncture-site inflammation with local care; culture/antibiotics only if spreading cellulitis or systemic signs

content:
Bone Marrow:
The bone marrow was normocellular with increased erythropoiesis and granulopoiesis, and megakaryopoiesis showing occasional juvenile forms. There was no excess of blasts or plasma cells, and no morphologic evidence of relapse, dysplasia, or fibrosis. Flow cytometry demonstrated a reduced B-cell compartment with a predominantly immature phenotype (CD10⁺/CD19⁺). Granulopoiesis appeared relatively increased with a normal maturation pattern. Approximately 2% CD34⁺/CD33⁺ myeloid blasts were detected, without evidence of lymphoid blasts or aberrant antigen expression. Initial immunophenotype at first diagnosis was: ETP-ALL: CD7+, CD34+, CD38+, TdT+, partly positive for CD33+ and CD117+ no CD1a, sCD3-, CD5 or CD8.

{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "extended_diagnostics: neuroimaging/venography follow-up (MRI/MRV or CT/CTV) and neurology consult note summarizing current status of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis and intracerebral hemorrhage, and anticoagulation recommendations."}}

response.request: extended_diagnostics: neuroimaging/venography follow-up (MRI/MRV or CT/CTV) and neurology consult note summarizing current status of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis and intracerebral hemorrhage, and anticoagulation recommendations.

content:
Extended Diagnostics:
ENT evaluation: Normal findings; no sinus or airway pathology. Gynecologic examination: Unremarkable; Pap smear negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy, HPV PCR negative. Ophthalmology: Normal ocular status, no retinopathy or CNS involvement. Thyroid ultrasound: Homogeneous parenchyma, no nodules, and normal vascularity. Dental status: Intact, no caries or chronic infectious focus identified.
Infection screen: Serum galactomannan <0.5; β-D-glucan not performed. CMV PCR negative. Rectal swab: Colonization with ESBL-producing Klebsiella pneumoniae (OXA-48 carbapenemase); MRSA negative, VRE positive. Immunology: ELISPOT negative.
Cardiology: ECG demonstrated normal sinus rhythm. Pulmonary function testing: Normal diffusion capacity (DLCO 88% predicted) and FEV₁ 91% predicted.
CSF analysis: Clear fluid, no elevated cell count, no malignant cells or blasts detected.
Brain MRI (06/15/2025): Demonstrated a small left parietal post-hemorrhagic lesion with hemosiderin deposition, consistent with residual changes following prior intracerebral hemorrhage. No acute bleeding, no new ischemic or demyelinating lesions, and no evidence of CNS leukemic involvement or meningeosis. Ventricular system and midline structures unremarkable.

solve.working_diagnosis: Early T-precursor T-ALL (ETP-ALL) in post-induction complete remission (marrow morphologic CR; CSF negative), complicated by prior cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with secondary intracerebral hemorrhage now with stable residual lesion and mild right arm weakness; on ongoing anticoagulation
differentials: Occult measurable residual disease (MRD-positive ALL) despite morphologic remission (needs formal MRD assay/report)

treatment_plan.action: Confirm depth of remission: obtain/verify formal MRD testing (multiparameter flow with leukemia-associated immunophenotype and/or NGS/TCR-based MRD) and full risk stratification to guide consolidation vs allogeneic HSCT decision

Proceed with protocol-based post-remission therapy for ETP-ALL (consolidation/intensification) and early transplant evaluation if MRD-positive/high-risk; arrange HLA typing and donor search; coordinate timing with neurology given prior ICH

Continue anticoagulation for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with neurology input; plan interval MRV/CTV to document recanalization; maintain platelet thresholds appropriate for therapeutic LMWH during intensive therapy and avoid agents increasing bleeding risk

Supportive care during consolidation: infection prophylaxis and contact precautions for ESBL/VRE colonization; monitor LFTs and adjust hepatotoxic meds/chemo as needed; neuro-rehabilitation and psychosocial counseling to address treatment adherence/consideration of discontinuation
If future CNS symptoms or relapse concern: repeat CSF and brain imaging promptly; otherwise routine surveillance per protocol

content:
Treatment History:
2025-03-02: The patient was newly diagnosed with early T-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ETP-ALL). A pre-phase regimen was initiated consisting of dexamethasone 10 mg/m² p.o. daily for 5 days and cyclophosphamide 200 mg/m² i.v. for 3 days, with good tolerability and rapid improvement of constitutional symptoms. A donor search was initiated, and an HLA-matched sibling was identified as a potential stem cell donor.
2025-03-10: Induction chemotherapy was started according to a standard adult ALL protocol. Dexamethasone p.o. 10 mg/m² (20 mg/day, divided into three doses) administered on days 6–7 and 13–16. Daunorubicin 45 mg/m² (86 mg i.v.) on days 6–7 and 13–14. Vincristine 2 mg i.v. (absolute dose) on days 6, 13, 20. PEG-asparaginase 500 U/m² (950 U absolute) on day 20. The regimen was tolerated initially; however, on day 17, the patient developed febrile neutropenia with Escherichia coli bacteremia, requiring intravenous meropenem 1 g q8h and short-course vancomycin (1.5 g q12h x 3 days) until cultures cleared. No hemodynamic instability occurred. Mild hyperglycemia was noted under steroids, managed conservatively.
2025-03-31: Bone marrow evaluation demonstrated blasts < 5%, consistent with hematologic remission. Intrathecal methotrexate 15 mg was administered on days 25, 32, and 39 as CNS prophylaxis. 6-Mercaptopurine 60 mg/m² (110 mg/day p.o.) was given on days 31–44. Cyclophosphamide 1000 mg/m² (1900 mg i.v.) was administered on days 24 and 44. Cytarabine 75 mg/m² (140 mg i.v.) was administered on days 26–29, 33–36, and 40–43. The patient experienced transient grade 2 mucositis and fatigue, but no neurotoxicity.
2025-04-14: CNS prophylaxis with cranial irradiation (24 Gy in fractionated doses) was initiated without acute toxicity.
2025-05-02: Bone marrow puncture after Induction I confirmed ongoing hematologic complete remission, pending molecular MRD assessment. 2025-05-14: The patient was discharged home in stable clinical condition.
2025-05-18: The patient was readmitted due to sudden severe headache and nausea. Cranial MRI revealed cerebral sinus venous thrombosis (superior sagittal sinus) with a small left-hemispheric intracerebral hemorrhage. She was admitted to the stroke unit for monitoring and received therapeutic-dose low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin 90mg q12h s.c.). Neurological symptoms improved gradually, leaving a mild residual weakness of the right arm. PEG-asparaginase therapy was considered the most likely contributing factor to the thrombotic event.
2025-05-27: After multidisciplinary discussion, consolidation chemotherapy was initiated with modifications to reduce CNS risk. Treatment consisted of: Dexamethasone 10 mg/m² (20 mg/day p.o., divided) on days 61–65. Vindesine 5mg on day 61. Methotrexate 1500 mg/m² (2850 mg i.v.) on day 61 with standard hydration and folinic acid rescue. Etoposide 250 mg/m² (475 mg i.v.) on days 64–65. Cytarabine 2000 mg/m² (3800 mg i.v. twice daily) on day 65. Intrathecal triple prophylaxis (methotrexate + cytarabine + hydrocortisone) was omitted due to the history of sinus venous thrombosis and prior intracerebral hemorrhage. The course was complicated by cytopenia (transfusion dependent) and grade 2 increase of liver entymes, but no infectious events or neurologic deterioration.
The patient was discharged for follow-up and neurological rehabilitation, with continuation of therapeutic anticoagulation and infection prophylaxis (valaciclovir, co-trimoxazole).

solve.working_diagnosis: Early T-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ETP-ALL) in hematologic complete remission after induction (blasts <5%) with post-induction consolidation started; complicated by PEG-asparaginase–associated cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (superior sagittal sinus) with secondary small intracerebral hemorrhage, now clinically stable with mild residual right arm weakness and on therapeutic LMWH; HLA-matched sibling donor identified
differentials: MRD-positive ETP-ALL (molecular/flow MRD status still not provided here and is decisive for transplant strategy)
CNS leukemia (currently unlikely given negative CSF and MRI without leukemic involvement)
Recurrent/progressive cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (needs venous imaging follow-up if symptoms/for stopping anticoagulation)

treatment_plan.action: Obtain and review the definitive MRD result (method, sensitivity, and timepoint) and convene transplant conference to finalize risk stratification (ETP-ALL + MRD-driven)

If MRD positive or high-risk features per protocol: proceed to allogeneic HSCT in first CR using the HLA-matched sibling donor as soon as medically feasible; use a non–asparaginase-containing bridging regimen if additional cytoreduction needed pre-HSCT; coordinate peri-transplant anticoagulation/platelet targets with neurology/stroke team given prior ICH

If MRD negative and standard-risk by protocol: continue protocol-based consolidation/intensification (avoiding further PEG-asparaginase given prior CVST/ICH) with planned CNS prophylaxis strategy individualized with neurology/radiation oncology (already received cranial RT; consider resumption of IT therapy only if/when bleeding risk acceptable)

Continue therapeutic-dose LMWH for CVST with planned interval MRV/CTV to document recanalization and define duration (typically at least 3–6 months and longer if ongoing prothrombotic risk/active therapy); maintain platelet transfusion thresholds appropriate for full-dose anticoagulation during cytopenic phases

Supportive care and safety: antimicrobial prophylaxis per ALL protocol; contact precautions and empiric antibiotic planning given ESBL/VRE colonization; manage transaminitis (avoid hepatotoxins, monitor LFTs closely during chemo/conditioning); neuro-rehabilitation and structured psychosocial support to address ambivalence about continuing curative-intent therapy
